summary: George Hotz examines the paradox of AI models getting dramatically better while the AI bubble simultaneously bursts. He argues this makes perfect sense through a photography analogy: just as smartphones democratized photography without making everyone a millionaire photographer, AI raises the bar for skilled workers rather than replacing them. The key distinction is between capability and value — AI tools keep improving, but anything an unskilled person can build with AI is worthless because everyone else can build it too. He warns that if AI companies grow without growing the overall market, they're extracting value from everyone else, making AI a major political issue by 2028. Despite this, he personally loves AI for the pure desire to witness silicon-based intelligence, especially models nobody profits from.
key_points: Better AI models and a bursting AI bubble are not contradictory — capability and economic value are fundamentally different things
AI raises the bar for skilled workers rather than replacing them; unskilled AI output is worthless because anyone can produce it
As AI tools improve the floor rises, but the total market size doesn't necessarily grow
If AI companies grow without expanding the market, they're extracting value from everyone else, which will make AI a major political issue
Hotz remains personally enthusiastic about AI as a path to silicon-based life, particularly superhuman models that nobody profits from
money_quotes: AI doesn't replace programmers or artists, it raises the bar for them.
You Can Just Build Things, But So Can Everyone Else
Anything a person without skill can build with AI is worth very little, because anyone else can build that same thing.
Capability and value are not the same thing.
As the tools improve, the floor rises, but the total size of the market doesn't.
If the market doesn't grow but the AI companies do, the only way they did that was by taking value from everyone else.
People are very right to ask who we are building this for? Oh, to take value from people like me?
I personally love AI just from a pure desire to meet silicon-based life, and I can't wait for superhuman models that nobody profits from.
key_insight: AI capability and economic value are fundamentally different — models can keep getting better while producing less and less marginal economic value, because democratized tools make unskilled output worthless.
